tools.name: get_dimension_hierarchies
tools.description:
Lists all hierarchies available within a dimension, along with the ordered levels in each hierarchy and their metadata. Use this first when you don't yet know what hierarchies or levels exist for a dimension. Each hierarchy row includes ``levelIdsPresent`` (true when Liquid Data returned native per-level ids) and ``levelSearchTokensPresent`` (true when each ``levels[].id`` is set — either from LD or, when ids are missing, copied from LD's ``levelName`` / ``dispLevelName`` / ``levelCaption`` for use in search_members ``dimension.levels``). Rows where ``id`` came from a name carry ``idFromLevelName: true``. Level **names** are required for get_descendants_at_level_members. Each hierarchy node also includes id, name, isLeaf, and memberFullPath, which can seed further drill-downs. Hierarchies whose ``id`` contains ``pending`` (any case) are omitted. Returns only ``retCode`` and a parsed ``hierarchies[]`` list; it does not echo the raw getDimensionDetails JSON. Does not return arbitrary members, so it is always safe to call (no member-count limits apply).

Inputs: modelId (string), dimensionName (e.g. "Product", "Geography"); optional numberOfLevelsToLoad.

loginDimension.description: Optional fields merged into the login request ``dimension`` object, e.g. memberFullPath, levelName, memberId (see LD security/login).

ldBaseUrl.description: Optional Advantage LD API origin (https://…, no trailing slash). When set, login and getDimensionDetails use this base instead of the server's LD_BASE_URL.

tools.name: get_descendants_at_level_members
tools.description:
Returns all descendants of a member that sit at a specific named level, skipping intermediate levels. Use this when the user wants a flat list at a known level ("all Categories under Beverages", "every Brand under Carbonated Soft Drinks"). Requires levelName — obtain valid level names from get_dimension_hierarchies first. The memberFullPath is the anchor (the subtree root); levelName is the target drill depth and must be deeper than the anchor's level. Contrast with get_members_children, which only goes one step down regardless of level.

**When to use this for resolution, not only drill-down:** When the user names a category-shaped concept that may not appear literally in member names (e.g. natural language like "carbonated beverages" versus an abbreviated code like "GE CARBNTD SFT DRNKS-390"), this tool can serve as a resolution strategy by anchoring at the **top of the hierarchy** and targeting the category-level ``levelName`` directly. That returns the full set of category names in the model (often a few hundred), which you can scan for fuzzy matches that substring search would miss.

Only use that top-down enumeration pattern when the hierarchy is **category-first** (no Brand, Manufacturer, Parent Company, or similar level **above** the target level in ``levels[]``) — otherwise the result can exceed 50,000 members and fail with retCode 32002. Check ``levels[]`` from get_dimension_hierarchies before deciding.

Size limit — important: any call whose result would contain ≥50,000 members fails outright with retCode 32002 ("This member contains more than 50000 descendants and cannot be listed"). Broad combinations like "all UPCs under an Aisle" or "all UPCs under Total Store" will reliably trip this. For normal drill-down, anchor as deeply as the user's intent allows — prefer Category or Subcategory over Aisle or Department when targeting leaf levels like UPC. If a call fails with 32002, narrow the anchor (drill one level down via get_members_children and retry) rather than re-issuing the same broad request.

Inputs: modelId (string), dimensionName, memberFullPath, levelName.
Response shape. Legacy LD JSON is preserved (``searchLevels`` / ``searchlevels`` with ``members[]``, ``memberFullPath``, level metadata). Also includes a top-level ``members`` array — each object has ``id``, ``fullpath``, and ``name`` — flattened from all hits.

memberFullPath.description: Subtree root anchor (fully-qualified path). Obtain valid level names from get_dimension_hierarchies first.
memberFullPath.example: Product.TOTAL STORE : FOLDER.TOTAL STORE.EDIBLE.DEPT-BEVERAGES.AISLE-CARBONATED SOFT DRINKS.CARBONATED BEVERAGES

levelName.description: Target named level for returned descendants (deeper than the anchor's level); must be a level name from get_dimension_hierarchies.

ldBaseUrl.description: Optional Advantage LD API origin for this request (https://…). Overrides the MCP server's LD_BASE_URL for login and getDescendantsAtLevelMembers.

tools.name: get_members_children
tools.description:
Returns the direct children of a single member — exactly one hierarchy step below memberFullPath. Use this for stepwise drill-down when the user is exploring ("what's under EDIBLE?", "show me the aisles in DEPT-BEVERAGES"). Does not accept a target level; if you need members at a specific deeper level, use get_descendants_at_level_members instead.
Size limit: if the direct children count is ≥50,000, the call fails with retCode 32002. This is rare for direct-children calls but possible for very wide nodes (e.g., a level holding tens of thousands of UPCs). If you hit this, drill via a more specific parent.

Inputs: modelId (string), dimensionName, memberFullPath. The memberFullPath must be the fully-qualified path including the dimension prefix and any folder/level segments, e.g. "Product.TOTAL STORE : FOLDER.TOTAL STORE.EDIBLE".
Response shape. Legacy LD JSON is preserved (``dimension.members[]``, ``memberFullPath``, level metadata). Also includes a top-level ``members`` array — each object has ``id``, ``fullpath``, and ``name`` — flattened from all child rows.

modelId.description: LD model id (sent as string in the LD JSON body).

memberFullPath.description: Full path of the parent member whose children are returned.

ldBaseUrl.description: Optional Advantage LD API origin for this request (https://…). Overrides the MCP server's LD_BASE_URL for login and getChildrenOfMember.

tools.name: search_members
tools.description:
Pattern-based search across dimension members. Use this when the user names a member or partial name ("find Pepsi", "products containing 'zero sugar'") rather than drilling structurally.
Use search when: the user provides a name, brand, or text fragment. Use drill tools when: the user provides a structural location.
When search returns **zero** matches or only **weak** matches, **do not** re-run the same search with a broader or shorter keyword, and **do not** fire many parallel variant searches — that saturates the 1,000-per-level cap and returns truncated, internal-ID-ordered noise. Treat a weak or empty result as a signal to change strategy (e.g. enumerate-then-match at a category-equivalent level when the hierarchy allows it) or to ask the user for a more specific name — not as a prompt to search again with noisier terms.
Response shape. Results are returned as searchLevels: [...], with one entry per hierarchy level that had matches. Each entry contains levelName, dispLevelName, memberCount (members actually returned, capped at 1,000), totalCount, and members[]. Each member has id, memberFullPath (renamed from LD's fullPath), ``fullpath`` (same path), and level metadata. A top-level ``members`` array is also included — each object has ``id``, ``fullpath``, and ``name`` — flattened from all level blocks (deduped by path).
Detecting truncation — read carefully. Each level is capped at 1,000 returned members. The truncation signal is totalCount > memberCount — when truncation occurs, totalCount is reported as 1001 (it does not reflect the true total; it just means "more than 1,000 exist"). If memberCount === 1000 and totalCount === 1000, the level genuinely has exactly 1,000 matches and is complete. If totalCount === 1001, the result is a partial sample of unknown size and should not be treated as authoritative — the returned 1,000 are an internal-ID-ordered slice, not the "best" or "most relevant" matches.
Operators. contains, like, startsWith, and endsWith are supported (prefer contains for substring search). Each keyword is matched as a whole substring, not tokenized — "Pepsi 12 Oz cans" only matches members containing that exact string with that exact spacing. There is no fuzzy match, stemming (can vs. cans), or word-order tolerance.
Multi-keyword search guidance — important. Do not try to construct precise multi-word queries. They will almost always miss results due to spacing, plurals, abbreviations, or word order. Instead:

Pick the single most distinctive keyword from the user's intent ("Pepsi", not "Pepsi 12 Oz cans").
Scope with level tokens when get_dimension_hierarchies shows ``levelSearchTokensPresent: true`` and each value is copied **verbatim** from ``levels[].id`` in that tool's JSON (native LD id, or the same string as ``levelName`` when ``idFromLevelName`` is true on that row — never construct tokens such as ``{hierarchyId}_Level_Brand``). Pass each such string in ``dimension.levels``.
Then filter or refine downstream — either by drilling under a returned member, by asking the user to pick from the candidate list, or by post-filtering the returned members in your own logic.
Avoid short common fragments ("12", "oz", "pe") — they saturate the 1,000-per-level cap and return noisy, truncated samples.

Always scope with level ids when the user's intent implies one tier ("find the Pepsi brand" → ``levels``: copy ``levels[].id`` for the row whose tool strings contain TRADEMARK or BRAND; "Pepsi UPCs" → id for the UPC row). Map English words (parent company, manufacturer, brand) to the closest prefetch row by substring — never pass ``Parent Company`` / ``Manufacturer`` / ``Brand`` unless that exact string is ``levels[].id``. Unscoped search hits every tier and is best reserved for genuine "I don't know where this lives" discovery. Use hierarchyId to disambiguate when a dimension has multiple hierarchies.

Inputs: modelId (string), and a dimension object, for example:
{
  "modelId": "2040",
  "dimension": {
    "name": "Product",
    "hierarchyId": "...",            // optional
    "levels": ["<verbatim levels[].id>", "..."],   // only when hierarchy has levelIdsPresent true
    "searchList": [
      { "keyword": "pepsi", "operator": "contains" }
    ]
  }
}
(LD ``searchMembers`` uses ``dimension.name`` for the dimension name, plus ``levels`` (level **ids**), ``searchList``, and optional ``hierarchyId``.)

ldBaseUrl.description: Optional Advantage LD API origin for this request (https://…). Overrides the MCP server's LD_BASE_URL for login and searchMembers.

dimension.description: LD searchMembers ``dimension`` object: ``name``, ``levels``, ``searchList`` (required); optional ``hierarchyId``, ``operator``, etc. See tool catalog description for truncation (1,000 per level), operators (contains, like, startsWith, endsWith), and keyword guidance.

levels.description: One or more strings copied verbatim from get_dimension_hierarchies ``levels[].id`` for the target hierarchy (native LD level id, or the same string as ``levelName`` when the tool set ``idFromLevelName`` on that row). Never invent tokens.

operator.description: LD comparison operator for the search (e.g. contains, like, startsWith).

hierarchyId.description: Optional hierarchy scope; null to omit preference.

tools.title: Search Members

tools.name: get_audience_metadata
tools.description: Returns the list of audience types for the selected client, with each type including the list of definitions available under that type. Use this catalog to choose the correct type and definition when building and scoping audiences while creating a size batch (create_size_batch).

clientId.description: Liquid Activation client UUID (sent as X-Client-Id). Defaults to e8d2abd2-ad45-4f49-9a48-a090349af1c7 when omitted.

tools.title: Get Audience Metadata

tools.name: get_batch_status
tools.description:
Poll the batch created for sizing or activation by checking its ``status`` field. While Liquid Activation is processing the job, ``status`` is typically ``processing``; when the run completes, it changes to ``sized``. Call repeatedly after create_size_batch until ``status`` is ``sized``. A batch may define more than one audience; each item in ``audiences[]`` reports its own ``status`` and, once sized, an ``estimateConsumerCount``. The batch-level ``status`` stays ``processing`` until every audience has completed sizing, then advances to ``sized``.

Expected run time depends on audience type. **Verified** sizing is usually quick — most batches complete in under two minutes. **ProScore** is a long-running estimate: allow roughly ten to sixty minutes depending on scope and platform load. Poll Verified batches at short intervals; for ProScore, use a slower, patient polling cadence and treat the job as asynchronous.

clientId.description: Liquid Activation client UUID (sent as X-Client-Id). Defaults to e8d2abd2-ad45-4f49-9a48-a090349af1c7 when omitted.

tools.title: Get Batch Status

tools.name: create_size_batch
tools.description:
Submit a size batch with one or more audiences for processing. Pass ``type=Size`` as a query parameter (default ``Size``); the request body carries ``clientId`` and the batch payload. The response ``id`` is the batch UUID — pass it as ``batchId`` to get_batch_status until ``status`` is ``sized``.

Each ``audienceMeasures[]`` entry must keep ``measureType``, ``measureId``, ``name``, and ``fullPath`` consistent. Use the matching tuple for the selected measure type:

- **Dollars** — ``measureId`` ``666048709``, ``name`` Dollar Sales, ``fullPath`` ``Measures.Household Groups.Dollar Sales``
- **Trips** — ``measureId`` ``666048694``, ``name`` Product Trips, ``fullPath`` ``Measures.Product Purchase : FOLDER.Shopper Dependent Measures.Product Trips``
- **Units** — ``measureId`` ``666048692``, ``name`` Unit Sales, ``fullPath`` ``Measures.Product Purchase : FOLDER.Unit Sales``

Set ``measureObj`` to the Liquid Data measure JSON for the same id/name/fullPath.

audienceMeasures.description:
Each ``audienceMeasures[]`` entry must keep ``measureType``, ``measureId``, ``name``, and ``fullPath`` consistent. Use the matching tuple for the selected measure type:

- **Dollars** — ``measureId`` ``666048709``, ``name`` Dollar Sales, ``fullPath`` ``Measures.Household Groups.Dollar Sales``
- **Trips** — ``measureId`` ``666048694``, ``name`` Product Trips, ``fullPath`` ``Measures.Product Purchase : FOLDER.Shopper Dependent Measures.Product Trips``
- **Units** — ``measureId`` ``666048692``, ``name`` Unit Sales, ``fullPath`` ``Measures.Product Purchase : FOLDER.Unit Sales``

Set ``measureObj`` to the Liquid Data measure JSON for the same id/name/fullPath.

fullPath.description: Liquid Data measure fullPath. Must match measureType — Dollars: 'Measures.Household Groups.Dollar Sales'; Trips: 'Measures.Product Purchase : FOLDER.Shopper Dependent Measures.Product Trips'; Units: 'Measures.Product Purchase : FOLDER.Unit Sales'.

measureId.description: Liquid Data measure id. Must match measureType — Dollars: 666048709; Trips: 666048694; Units: 666048692.

measureObj.description: JSON string for the same measure (id, name, fullPath, measureType) as measureId.

measureType.description: Measure family. Use the matching measureId, name, and fullPath: Dollars → 666048709 / Dollar Sales / Measures.Household Groups.Dollar Sales; Trips → 666048694 / Product Trips / Measures.Product Purchase : FOLDER.Shopper Dependent Measures.Product Trips; Units → 666048692 / Unit Sales / Measures.Product Purchase : FOLDER.Unit Sales.

name.description: Measure display name. Must match measureType — Dollars: Dollar Sales; Trips: Product Trips; Units: Unit Sales.

examples.fullPath: Measures.Product Purchase : FOLDER.Shopper Dependent Measures.Product Trips
examples.name: Product Trips

audienceProducts.fullPath: Product.All Products.TOTAL STORE.EDIBLE.DEPT-BEVERAGES.AISLE-CARBONATED SOFT DRINKS.CARBONATED BEVERAGES.LOW CALORIE SOFT DRINKS.COCA COLA CO LOW CALORIE SOFT DRINKS
audienceProducts.productName: COCA COLA CO LOW CALORIE SOFT DRINKS

batchType.description: Batch type forwarded to POST /batches?type=Size. May also be passed as the HTTP query parameter ``type=Size``.

clientId.description: Liquid Activation client UUID (sent as X-Client-Id).
